generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotorActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "The physical actuation system comprises a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis of the robot."
]

instance SiemensSinumerik840DSLController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "The control mechanism is implemented via Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl system, enabling precise motion regulation."
]

instance SafetyCellConstraint : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Safety constraints are enforced by a restricted working range within a safety cell, limiting operational limits to prevent collisions."
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance MotorPositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder mounted directly on the motor for real-time angle measurement"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorAngleDataTransmitted
]

instance GearboxPositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder mounted behind the gearbox for additional angle measurement"
    DTDFVocab:producedData GearboxAngleDataTransmitted
]

instance CurrentSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Input current sensor measuring electrical parameters of the drive system"
    DTDFVocab:producedData CurrentDataTransmitted
]

// C4: Data transmission
instance MotorAngleDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorPositionEncoder
]

instance GearboxAngleDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom GearboxPositionEncoder
]

instance CurrentDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom CurrentSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance RobotDynamicSimulinkModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based dynamic simulation model using Simulink for robot motion analysis"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MathWorksSimulinkEnabler
]

instance FrictionLossIdentificationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Data-driven model for continuous friction loss identification using probabilistic latent variable models"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MathWorksSimulinkEnabler
]

instance DataTraceAggregationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Model for aggregating and abstracting time-series data traces from physical sensors"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MathWorksSimulinkEnabler
]

instance DataTraceProcessingModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simulation model for processing raw data traces into higher-level abstractions"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MathWorksSimulinkEnabler
]

instance PhysicalRobotModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Intrinsic robot model for scalable identification of physical characteristics"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SorrentinoIntrinsicModelEnabler
]

instance ConformanceValidationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Model ensuring digital shadow conforms to reference model specifications"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo UMLClassDiagramEnabler
]

instance DataTraceMetadataModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Metadata model supporting traceability and further processing of data traces"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLMetadataEnabler
]

instance RobotPerformanceDashboardData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated performance data for real-time monitoring of joint friction and system performance"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorAngleDataTransmitted, GearboxAngleDataTransmitted, CurrentDataTransmitted
]

instance FrictionLossTraceData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Continuous data trace for friction loss identification in robot operations"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorAngleDataTransmitted, CurrentDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo FrictionLossIdentificationModel
]

// C11: Enablers
instance MathWorksSimulinkEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation tool environment for processing and modeling robot dynamics using Simulink"
    DTDFVocab:enables RobotDynamicSimulinkModel, DataTraceProcessingModel, DataTraceAggregationModel
]

instance SorrentinoIntrinsicModelEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Framework for scalable identification of intrinsic robot models (Sorrentino et al.)"
    DTDFVocab:enables PhysicalRobotModel
]

instance UMLClassDiagramEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "UML-based framework for defining reference models and conformance relations"
    DTDFVocab:enables ConformanceValidationModel
]

instance SysMLMetadataEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML methodology for creating and managing metadata for traceability"
    DTDFVocab:enables DataTraceMetadataModel
]

instance MQTTProtocolEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT protocol implementation for real-time data transmission between physical and digital systems"
    DTDFVocab:enables MotorAngleDataTransmitted, GearboxAngleDataTransmitted, CurrentDataTransmitted
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Dashboard visualization service for real-time monitoring of joint friction and system performance"
    DTDFVocab:provides DashboardVisualizationInsight
]

instance FrictionAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous multi-physics-aware identification of friction loss through aggregated data traces"
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionLossInsight, AnomalyDetectionInsight
]

instance MotionAccuracyService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service for continuous monitoring and optimization of motion accuracy in robot operations"
    DTDFVocab:provides AccuracyMetricsInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance DashboardVisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time visualization of robot performance metrics displayed on the dashboard"
]

instance FrictionLossInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Identified friction loss values derived from processed data traces"
]

instance AnomalyDetectionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Detected anomalies in operational behavior through friction loss patterns"
]

instance AccuracyMetricsInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Calculated accuracy metrics for robot motion performance"
]

instance PerformanceOptimizationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated adjustment of control parameters based on identified friction loss patterns"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance AnomalyResponseAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Triggered actions when anomalies are detected in operational behavior"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance CalibrationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Scheduled calibration procedures based on accumulated performance data"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// Environment
instance RobotSystemEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotorActuator,
        SiemensSinumerik840DSLController,
        SafetyCellConstraint,
        MotorPositionEncoder,
        GearboxPositionEncoder,
        CurrentSensor,
        RobotDynamicSimulinkModel,
        FrictionLossIdentificationModel,
        DataTraceProcessingModel,
        PhysicalRobotModel,
        MathWorksSimulinkEnabler,
        MQTTProtocolEnabler,
        RealTimeMonitoringService,
        FrictionAnalysisService,
        MotionAccuracyService
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "A Digital Twin system in the provided context operates with **asynchronous, one-way data flow** between physical and digital components, where the Digital Shadow (DS) functions as a **passive entity** with no autonomous data-pushing capabilities (Kritzinger et al., 2018). The **temporal granularity** is determined by the **data trace collection frequency** from the physical system, which is not explicitly quantified but is conceptualized as a **purpose-driven aggregation** of contextual data traces (Document 1). For Digital Shadows, the **processing logic** (e.g., data calculation or simulation models) ensures traceability of input/output data traces, but the **latency requirements** or **synchronization frequencies** between physical and digital components are not detailed in the documents. The **reference model** allows for **customizable time scales** for different services, but no specific tools or methodologies (e.g., Simulink, C++, Java) are linked to time-scale management in the twinning process. Thus, the **twinning_time_scale** is implicitly tied to the **data trace collection rate** and **processing speed** of the Digital Shadow’s components, but no concrete time-scale specifications (e.g., real-time, near-real-time, or batch processing) are provided in the documents."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **Digital Twin (DT) constellation** is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Document 1) describes a **passive, one-way data flow** between physical and digital entities, implying a **modular, component-based architecture** where Digital Shadows (DS) are **customizable** for specific purposes. The **concretization** of the reference model (e.g., mapping to UML Class Diagrams) suggests **orchestration via conformance relations**, but no **architecture patterns** (e.g., microservices, event-driven), **component integration methods** (e.g., SysML, AADL), or **resource management** (e.g., cloud vs. edge deployment) are specified. The **processing components** (e.g., data calculation/simulation models) are encoded in tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (C++, Java), but their role in **system-wide coordination** is not elaborated. Thus, **dt_constellation** lacks concrete technical details in the documents."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
  **Not explicitly detailed in the provided documents**, but inferred from broader Industry 4.0 and digital twin literature:
  - **Development Phase**:
    - **Requirements & Conceptualization**: Define purpose-driven or data-driven digital shadow/twin design (Document 2).
    - **Modeling & Abstraction**: Use MBSE (Model-Based Systems Engineering) methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SysML, ADLs) to create layered abstractions (requirements → functional → logical → technical architecture) (Document 1).
    - **Data Collection & Processing**: Aggregate contextual data traces (Document 1 & 2) and incorporate metadata for traceability (Document 2).
  - **Operational Phase**:
    - **Real-time Synchronization**: One-way data flow from physical systems to digital shadows (passive) or bidirectional interaction in digital twins (active components for autonomy) (Document 1).
    - **Continuous Refinement**: Adaptation via simulation/data calculation models (Document 2) to support higher-level abstractions (e.g., production planning, optimization) (Document 5).
  - **Lifecycle Governance**:
    - **Conformance Relations**: Reference models (e.g., UML class diagrams) define domain concepts and relationships, ensuring conformance to standards (Document 2).
    - **Customization**: Concrete realizations (e.g., injection molding DS) adapt reference models to specific use cases (Document 2).
  - **Transition Management**: Methodologies like those for defining digital twins (Document 2) integrate DS and DT evolution, though specifics are not detailed.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
    **Model fidelity** in this Digital Twin/Digital Shadow framework is ensured through:
    - **Hybrid modeling**: Combining **physics-based (white-box) models** (e.g., Simulink simulations) for predictability with **data-driven (black-box) models** (e.g., machine learning) to capture nonlinearities. This hybrid approach addresses **traditional static models’ limitations** (e.g., inability to represent complex relationships).
    - **Validation/verification methods**: Research highlights **traceability and correctness** via model-based development, including **SysML-to-simulation transformations** to verify data/model transformations. Traceability ensures **transitive integrity**—e.g., verifying computed traces derive from accurate input data and processing steps.
    - **Uncertainty quantification**: Implicitly relies on **scalable identification methods** (e.g., Sorrentino et al.) to minimize setup errors, though explicit sources of uncertainty (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) are not detailed. **Quality assurance** is implied through **conformance relations** in reference models (e.g., ensuring concrete DS models adhere to domain-specific standards).
    The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) underscores that **fidelity depends on data trace correctness** and **processing logic accuracy**, with no explicit error correction mechanisms mentioned. However, the **purpose-driven design** of DS models (e.g., for industrial robot friction) suggests iterative validation loops to refine accuracy.
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** is implemented via a **UML class diagram**, defining core concepts for manufacturing digital shadows. Communication relies on **MQTT protocol** for data streaming, ensuring lightweight and efficient data exchange. While the document does not specify network topology, it implies a **distributed architecture** supporting real-time data aggregation. Processing components utilize **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation tools for computational logic, maintaining traceability through model-driven associations. No explicit mention of **bandwidth/latency requirements** or **reliability/redundancy mechanisms** is provided, but the model supports **conformance relations** for customizable conforming models.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "The documents emphasize **Digital Shadows** as a structured method for capturing cyber-physical system data traces, but **no explicit security protocols, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms** are detailed. The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)**—a foundational component in Industry 4.0—is referenced in Document 50 (Platform Industrie 4.0, 2019) as a semantic framework for asset modeling, but its security implications are not elaborated. However, **robot safety** is implicitly addressed via the **MABI robotic arm (Document 44)**, which operates within a structured safety cell environment, ensuring physical protection during operation. The **conformance relation** in reference models (Document 41) supports structured validation of Digital Shadow implementations but does not inherently enforce cybersecurity or safety compliance. Thus, while traceability and provenance are critical, **no explicit security protocols (e.g., MQTT, Thingsboard, or IoT frameworks like Document 53) or fail-safe mechanisms** are documented within the provided context."
]
